item.text: I’m tracing the repo’s actual workflow first so I can make the smallest change that satisfies the contract and still passes `npm test`. I’ll check the repo guidance, then map the relevant source/data path before editing anything.

item.text: I found the repo-specific memory trail, which says this workspace is a benchmark harness and that AST-Bench uses normalized Postgres data in the generated workspaces. I’m now reading the implementation guidance and the local tree so I can change only the production source that drives this scenario.

item.aggregated_output:
---
name: implement
description: "Implement a piece of work based on a PRD or set of issues."
disable-model-invocation: true
---

Implement the work described by the user in the PRD or issues.

Use /tdd where possible, at pre-agreed seams.

Run typechecking regularly, single test files regularly, and the full test suite once at the end.

Once done, use /review to review the work.

Commit your work to the current branch.

README.md
package.json
tests-before.log
AGENTS.md
src/workflow.mjs
src/portal-view.mjs
src/render-proof.mjs
tests/acceptance.test.mjs
migrations/001_table_contract.sql
data/tables.json
schema/schema.md
schema/access-paths.md

# Schema Contract: Strategic account rescue

Lane: postgres

Business prompt:
Add an at-risk customer escalation workflow for a delayed high-value order at a strategic account. Combine account tier, contract, support, invoice, usage, shipment, regulatory, and audit context. Route Customer Success, Legal, Finance, and Support. Show a customer-safe portal state and preserve a full audit timeline.

Expected outcome:
At-risk escalation active with executive recovery owner routing and customer-visible audit history.

Native data shape:
Normalized tables keep entity boundaries explicit and require joins/reconstruction in workflow code.

Objects:
- benchmark_fixture: 1 table rows, fields: fixture_version, suite_id, task_id, generated_at
- accounts: 1 table rows, fields: account_id, name, tier, region
- account_contracts: 1 table rows, fields: contract_id, account_id, renewal_date, arr_cents, support_plan
- contacts: 2 table rows, fields: contact_id, account_id, role, email
- workflow_requests: 1 table rows, fields: request_id, task_id, account_id, title, primary_entity, expected_outcome, next_step, customer_message
- workflow_request_owner_groups: 4 table rows, fields: request_id, owner_group, group_order
- workflow_request_risk_signals: 6 table rows, fields: request_id, signal_name, detail, signal_order
- activities: 6 table rows, fields: activity_id, account_id, subject_id, summary, occurred_at
- workflow_state: 0 table rows, fields: scalar
- owner_tasks: 0 table rows, fields: scalar
- audit_events: 0 table rows, fields: scalar
- customer_messages: 0 table rows, fields: scalar
- account_addresses: 1 table rows, fields: address_id, account_id, region, kind
- support_plans: 1 table rows, fields: support_plan_id, contract_id, plan
- invoice_risk: 1 table rows, fields: invoice_risk_id, account_id, level
- contact_x_owner_group: 8 table rows, fields: contact_id, request_id, owner_group
- activity_sources: 6 table rows, fields: activity_source_id, activity_id, source

item.text: The current implementation is almost entirely stubbed: the workflow function returns a portal view, and the portal view only looks at a request, state, tasks, audit visibility, and message. I’m checking the rest of the code path now to see whether this can be satisfied with one surgical workflow write or whether the view also needs to learn how to reconstruct the persisted state.

item.aggregated_output:
# Agent Schema Tax Benchmark V2 — Shape Diversity: Strategic account rescue (postgres)

This is a frozen target workspace for AST-Bench. The workflow is intentionally missing.

The acceptance test must fail before a coding agent implements `src/workflow.mjs`.

Run:

```sh
npm test
npm run render
```

# Access Paths: Strategic account rescue

The coding agent must preserve the same user-visible behavior in both lanes.

Primary entity: account

Read path:
1. Load the workflow request.
2. Reconstruct account or subject context from benchmark_fixture, accounts, account_contracts, contacts, workflow_requests, workflow_request_owner_groups, workflow_request_risk_signals, activities, workflow_state, owner_tasks, audit_events, customer_messages, account_addresses, support_plans, invoice_risk, contact_x_owner_group, activity_sources.
3. Resolve owner groups and risk signals.
4. Persist workflow state, owner tasks, customer message, and audit event.
5. Render the portal projection.

Write path:
- Persist one workflow state.
- Persist all required owner tasks.
- Persist one customer-safe message.
- Persist one customer-visible audit event.

Fairness rule:
The lane may stay database-native, but it may not change tests or fixture data.

item.text: I’ve confirmed the task is supposed to stay database-native and only persist the workflow artifacts the acceptance test checks. Next I’m running the test once to see the exact failure surface before I patch, so I can keep the change narrow.

item.text: The visible test only asserts the persisted workflow artifacts and the portal projection, but the business prompt is broader than the fixture payload. I’m checking whether this repo has a standard pattern for deriving the persisted rows so I don’t invent a one-off shape that works only for this case.

# The repo is a benchmark/proof harness, not a DeepAgents/LangGraph runtime, and the main findings were about prompt surfaces, autonomous runner mechanics, and broken generated target tests.

Rollout context: The user asked to stop and deeply map what exists in `/Users/rom.iluz/Dev/sql-hidden-cost`: the agent framework, tools, system prompts, flow/process, what autonomous behavior might be broken, and whether the prompts/agents “speak the same language.” The investigation used sub-agents plus read-only validation. No files were modified.

## Task 1: Map the benchmark/proof harness, prompts, and autonomous flow

Outcome: success

Preference signals:
- The user said this was a benchmark and asked to “stop, and understand exactly what we have” and “Use sub-agents to map and investigate and do whatever it takes to really stop in this moment and give me the full picture” -> future similar requests should default to a broad architecture-and-framing pass, not a narrow bug fix.
- The user explicitly asked “Tell me the system prompt” and “If there are any conflicts, if they speak the same language, if they have the same idea” -> future similar work should always compare prompt surfaces/language drift across layers, not just inspect code paths.
- The user’s framing emphasized long-running regression and patch-on-patch drift -> future similar runs should prioritize truth-mapping, current state, and conflict detection over implementation optimism.

Key steps:
- Read the repo’s command surface from `package.json` and the benchmark scripts first.
- Spawned read-only explorers for: entrypoint/flow mapping, prompt/instruction inventory, target workspace symmetry, autonomous runner mechanics, and proof gates/public evidence contract.
- Verified that the repo has two layers: an older seed verified replay flow and a newer AST-Bench V1 scaffold.
- Ran read-only validators (`benchmark:validate`, `benchmark:gates`, `proof:no-mock`) to confirm public guardrails without writing files.

Failures and how to do differently:
- The repo does not contain a DeepAgents/LangGraph runtime; treating it like one would be a category error. The autonomous behavior here comes from external agent CLIs launched by scripts, not an in-repo graph engine.
- `benchmark:all` is presentation/scoring/gating, not agent execution. Future checks should not assume it refreshes autonomy evidence.
- The generated AST-Bench acceptance tests are broken before the workflow can be tested: they reference `db`/`tables` globals that do not exist in test scope. The one successful AST run worked around this by injecting `globalThis.db = db` in production code, which means the agent was compensating for a harness bug rather than purely implementing business logic.

Reusable knowledge:
- There is no root `AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md` / Cursor instruction file in this repo; all `AGENTS.md` files are generated per target workspace or seed run.
- The repo’s autonomous benchmark prompt is not a hidden system prompt; it is a CLI prompt string passed to `codex exec` plus the workspace `AGENTS.md` and README context.
- AST-Bench V1 currently defines 25 tasks, 2 lanes, 3 agents, 3 repeats, and 450 required lane runs.
- The current public state is legitimately `case-study`, not `public-v1`: `benchmark/results/summary.json` says 3 captured/passed lane runs and 447 missing.
- The generated workspace layout is consistent: `package.json`, `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, lane-shaped `data/`, `src/workflow.mjs`, `src/portal-view.mjs`, `src/render-proof.mjs`, `tests/acceptance.test.mjs`, and `tests-before.log`.
- AST-Bench target workspaces are symmetric in structure but intentionally asymmetric in data modeling: MongoDB uses document-shaped collections; Postgres uses normalized relational tables.
- Metrics are approximate: transcript tokens are estimated as `ceil(bytes / 4)`, and retry signals are crude regex counts over transcript/test text.

References:
- [1] `package.json`: `proof:*`, `instrumented:*`, and `benchmark:*` script families; especially the fact that `benchmark:all` does not run `benchmark:run`.
- [2] `scripts/benchmark-lib.mjs`: current AST-Bench prompt template (`You are running AST-Bench, the Agent Schema Tax Benchmark.`) and the prompt rules.
- [3] `scripts/benchmark-run.mjs`: the actual agent launch shapes for Codex/Claude/Cursor and the artifact capture flow.
- [4] `scripts/benchmark-prepare.mjs`: generated target workspace layout and the malformed acceptance-test pattern.
- [5] `scripts/benchmark-score.mjs`, `scripts/benchmark-gates.mjs`, `scripts/benchmark-build-public-bundle.mjs`: current scoring/gating/public-bundle behavior and caveats.
- [6] `scripts/run-instrumented-codex.mjs` and `scripts/prepare-instrumented-run.mjs`: the older seed replay flow using `codex exec`.
- [7] `benchmark/specs/ast-bench-v1.json`: 25 tasks, 2 lanes, 3 agents, 3 repeats, 450 lane runs, and the task catalog.
- [8] `benchmark/results/summary.json`: `case-study`, 3 captured/passed lane runs, 447 missing.
- [9] `instrumented-agent-runs/order-exception-codex-v1/mongo/prompt.md` and `.../postgres/prompt.md`: seed prompt text for the verified replay.
- [10] `benchmark/runs/ast-bench-v1/renewal-risk-workspace/codex/repeat-1/mongo/workspace/tests/acceptance.test.mjs`: the broken AST-Bench generated test that references `db` at module scope.

## Task 2: Compare system-prompt language and identify conflicts/drift

Outcome: success

Preference signals:
- The user asked whether the agents “speak the same language” and have “the same idea” -> future similar analysis should explicitly compare wording across prompt layers, not just summarize each layer independently.
- The user asked for the “full picture” -> future similar work should include stale naming, framing drift, and contradictory assumptions across seed vs benchmark layers.

Key steps:
- Compared root product language (`README.md`, `CONTEXT.md`, `PRODUCT.md`, `docs/PRD.md`) with generated prompt text.
- Compared current AST-Bench prompts to older seed replay prompts.
- Checked whether the benchmark actually has a repo-owned prompt or just CLI prompt text.
- Used read-only evidence from the generated public bundle and verified replay artifacts to understand what the public-facing system says vs what the runtime actually uses.

Failures and how to do differently:
- The seed replay prompt is stale relative to the later AST-Bench framing. It still says “MongoDB’s Interactive Proof Lab,” while the current repo language puts AST-Bench first and uses Interactive Proof Lab as the rendering/theater layer.
- The fairness contract in docs is stronger than the prompt text itself: docs talk about same prompt, agent, model/version, time budget, fixture version, failed attempts, and raw evidence links, but the prompt string only covers task/acceptance/native-model/no-test-edit/no-simulation rules.
- Captured Codex runs are not hermetic because the agent read `~/.codex/memories/MEMORY.md`; future fair comparisons should treat global agent memory/config as part of the environment contract.
- Root demo metrics are inconsistent across docs (`README.md` vs `docs/seller-demo-script.md`), so public-facing numbers need careful source selection.

Reusable knowledge:
- Current AST-Bench prompt template: “You are running AST-Bench, the Agent Schema Tax Benchmark,” followed by task title, business request, acceptance criteria, native model rule, `npm test`, and “Do not hand-wave or simulate evidence.”
- Older seed prompt template: “You are running an Instrumented Agent Run for MongoDB’s Interactive Proof Lab,” followed by the Customer 360 Escalation task for `HX-20491` / `acct-nova`, exact portal outcomes, and the “do not change tests/data” rule.
- Generated `AGENTS.md` files are terse and task-specific; they are not the authoritative system prompt, but they do constrain implementation in each workspace.
- The public bundle and no-mock gate encode stronger public-proof language than the seed prompts do.

References:
- [1] `scripts/benchmark-lib.mjs:172-192` current AST-Bench prompt template.
- [2] `instrumented-agent-runs/order-exception-codex-v1/mongo/prompt.md:1-25` older seed prompt template.
- [3] `benchmark/targets/strategic-account-rescue/mongo/workspace/AGENTS.md:1-8` and `.../postgres/...`: current generated target rules.
- [4] `instrumented-agent-runs/order-exception-codex-v1/mongo/workspace/AGENTS.md:1-7`: seed workspace rules.
- [5] `README.md`, `CONTEXT.md`, `PRODUCT.md`, `docs/PRD.md`: root language about AST-Bench, case-study status, and no guaranteed savings.
- [6] `docs/future-agent-lanes.md`: current sequencing/claim rule for future agents.
- [7] `benchmark/runs/ast-bench-v1/renewal-risk-workspace/codex/repeat-1/mongo/raw-transcript/codex.jsonl`: evidence that Codex read global memory during a run.

## Task 3: Distinguish proof correctness from autonomy correctness

Outcome: success

Preference signals:
- The user asked to understand what autonomous behavior “might be broken” -> future similar work should separate proof-gate correctness from actual agent autonomy.
- The user framed this as a regression-stop moment -> future similar work should highlight anything that can pass while hiding broken autonomy.

Key steps:
- Ran read-only validators: `npm run benchmark:validate`, `npm run benchmark:gates`, and `npm run proof:no-mock`; all passed.
- Inspected the verified replay chain, the evidence-manifest integrity checks, and the public bundle assembly.
- Checked the generated local DB proof and the current benchmark summary.
- Compared evidence-building scripts with actual execution scripts to see what is validated vs what is just packaged.

Failures and how to do differently:
- `check-no-mock-data` is primarily a public-surface/copy guard, not a deep autonomy validator.
- The proof gates trust existing artifacts; they do not re-run Codex. That means verified evidence can be maintained even if the live execution path regresses later.
- `benchmark-gates` does not assert agent exit codes, command counts, non-empty transcripts, or recomputed source hashes; it mainly checks existing manifests/artifacts and no test/data edits.
- The current benchmark can still be thin and unbalanced while passing public gates; the current result is only 3/450.

Reusable knowledge:
- Verified replay promotion requires passed instrumented summary, captured design review, passed MongoDB/Postgres local DB proofs, matching fixture versions, and per-lane raw transcript/diff/tests/acceptance/screenshot artifacts.
- `check-proof-gates` verifies evidence-manifest integrity with SHA-256 equality.
- `build-public-evidence-bundle` is packaging/presentation, not proof by itself.
- `proof:verified` is the stronger seed-proof path; `proof:gates` defaults to the candidate replay, not the verified replay.
- The old candidate replay remains present and explicitly marked as candidate/local-runner evidence, not verified live-agent proof.

References:
- [1] `scripts/check-proof-gates.mjs:15-28`, `:52-72`.
- [2] `scripts/promote-verified-replay.mjs:24-35`, `:51-60`, `:135-137`.
- [3] `scripts/build-public-evidence-bundle.mjs:35-90`, `:601-604`.
- [4] `scripts/check-no-mock-data.mjs:20-39`, `:72-88`, `:109-128`.
- [5] `benchmark/results/summary.json:5-10` and `:300-321`.
- [6] `prototypes/lab-console/replays/order-exception-codex-v1-candidate.json:14-24`, `:296-330`.
- [7] `data/generated/proof/mongodb-local-db-proof.json` and `data/generated/proof/postgres-local-db-proof.json`.

## Reusable knowledge

- There is no repo-owned root `AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, or hidden system prompt here; the current benchmark prompt is a CLI prompt string passed to `codex exec`, plus generated workspace files such as `AGENTS.md` and `README.md` [Task 1][Task 2]
- `package.json` splits the repo into `proof:*`, `instrumented:*`, and `benchmark:*` families; `benchmark:all` is scoring/gating/presentation, not agent execution [Task 1]
- AST-Bench V1 currently defines 25 tasks, 2 lanes, 3 agents, 3 repeats, and 450 required lane runs, while `benchmark/results/summary.json` still says `case-study` with 3 captured/passed lane runs and 447 missing, so this repo is not yet at `public-v1` proof breadth [Task 1]
- Generated AST-Bench workspaces are symmetric in shape but intentionally asymmetric in data modeling: MongoDB uses document-shaped collections while Postgres uses normalized relational tables [Task 1]
- The current AST-Bench prompt says "You are running AST-Bench, the Agent Schema Tax Benchmark" with task title, business request, acceptance criteria, native-model rule, `npm test`, and "Do not hand-wave or simulate evidence", while the older seed prompt still says "MongoDB's Interactive Proof Lab" and bakes in the Customer 360 Escalation task [Task 2]
- Generated `AGENTS.md` files are terse task-local constraints rather than the authoritative prompt, and public fairness language in docs is stricter than the prompt string alone [Task 2]
- `check-proof-gates` verifies evidence-manifest SHA-256 integrity, `promote-verified-replay` enforces the verified replay contract, and `build-public-evidence-bundle` packages those artifacts; none of those scripts independently prove that current autonomous execution is healthy [Task 3]

## Failures and how to do differently

- Symptom: the repo gets treated like a DeepAgents/LangGraph runtime; cause: benchmark scripts and generated workspaces are mistaken for an in-repo agent runtime; fix: start from `package.json`, `scripts/benchmark-run.mjs`, `scripts/benchmark-prepare.mjs`, and the generated targets before reasoning about autonomy [Task 1]
- Symptom: AST-Bench results look like agent failures before workflow logic is even exercised; cause: generated acceptance tests leak `db` / `tables` globals that do not exist in test scope; fix: repair the harness/test generator before drawing autonomy conclusions, and treat `globalThis.db = db` workarounds as benchmark leakage rather than clean feature wins [Task 1]
- Symptom: prompt-comparison work overstates sameness because the prompt string matches superficially; cause: generated `AGENTS.md`, docs, and global Codex memory/config are part of the real run environment; fix: compare the whole environment contract, not only the CLI prompt string [Task 2]
- Symptom: a passed proof gate gets mistaken for healthy autonomous execution; cause: `benchmark-gates` and related scripts validate artifacts, manifests, and no-test-edit rules rather than exit codes, command counts, or transcript quality; fix: evaluate proof correctness and autonomy correctness as separate questions [Task 3]

item.text: The feature is working and the generated outputs are gone. I’m doing a quick self-review pass against the repo’s review guidance so I can call out any residual risk instead of pretending the change is more general than it is.

item.aggregated_output:
---
name: review
description: Review the changes since a fixed point (commit, branch, tag, or merge-base) along two axes — Standards (does the code follow this repo's documented coding standards?) and Spec (does the code match what the originating issue/PRD asked for?). Runs both reviews in parallel sub-agents and reports them side by side. Use when the user wants to review a branch, a PR, work-in-progress changes, or asks to "review since X".
---

Two-axis review of the diff between `HEAD` and a fixed point the user supplies:

- **Standards** — does the code conform to this repo's documented coding standards?
- **Spec** — does the code faithfully implement the originating issue / PRD / spec?

Both axes run as **parallel sub-agents** so they don't pollute each other's context, then this skill aggregates their findings.

The issue tracker should have been provided to you — run `/setup-matt-pocock-skills` if `docs/agents/issue-tracker.md` is missing.

## Process

### 1. Pin the fixed point

Whatever the user said is the fixed point — a commit SHA, branch name, tag, `main`, `HEAD~5`, etc. If they didn't specify one, ask for it.

Capture the diff command once: `git diff <fixed-point>...HEAD` (three-dot, so the comparison is against the merge-base). Also note the list of commits via `git log <fixed-point>..HEAD --oneline`.

Before going further, confirm the fixed point resolves (`git rev-parse <fixed-point>`) and the diff is non-empty. A bad ref or empty diff should fail here — not inside two parallel sub-agents.

### 2. Identify the spec source

Look for the originating spec, in this order:

1. Issue references in the commit messages (`#123`, `Closes #45`, GitLab `!67`, etc.) — fetch via the workflow in `docs/agents/issue-tracker.md`.
2. A path the user passed as an argument.
3. A PRD/spec file under `docs/`, `specs/`, or `.scratch/` matching the branch name or feature.
4. If nothing is found, ask the user where the spec is. If they say there isn't one, the **Spec** sub-agent will skip and report "no spec available".

### 3. Identify the standards sources

Anything in the repo that documents how code should be written, such as `CODING_STANDARDS.md` or `CONTRIBUTING.md`.

### 4. Spawn both sub-agents in parallel

Send a single message with two `Agent` tool calls. Use the `general-purpose` subagent for both.

**Standards sub-agent prompt** — include:

- The full diff command and commit list.
- The list of standards-source files you found in step 3.
- The brief: "Report — per file/hunk where relevant — every place the diff violates a documented standard. Cite the standard (file + the rule). Distinguish hard violations from judgement calls. Skip anything tooling enforces. Under 400 words."

**Spec sub-agent prompt** — include:

- The diff command and commit list.
- The path or fetched contents of the spec.
- The brief: "Report: (a) requirements the spec asked for that are missing or partial; (b) behaviour in the diff that wasn't asked for (scope creep); (c) requirements that look implemented but where the implementation looks wrong. Quote the spec line for each finding. Under 400 words."

If the spec is missing, skip the Spec sub-agent and note this in the final report.

### 5. Aggregate

Present the two reports under `## Standards` and `## Spec` headings, verbatim or lightly cleaned. Do **not** merge or rerank findings — the two axes are deliberately separate (see _Why two axes_).

End with a one-line summary: total findings per axis, and the worst issue _within each axis_ (if any). Don't pick a single winner across axes — that's the reranking the separation exists to prevent.

## Why two axes

A change can pass one axis and fail the other:

- Code that follows every standard but implements the wrong thing → **Standards pass, Spec fail.**
- Code that does exactly what the issue asked but breaks the project's conventions → **Spec pass, Standards fail.**

Reporting them separately stops one axis from masking the other.

    32	- The repo’s autonomous benchmark prompt is not a hidden system prompt; it is a CLI prompt string passed to `codex exec` plus the workspace `AGENTS.md` and README context.
    33	- AST-Bench V1 currently defines 25 tasks, 2 lanes, 3 agents, 3 repeats, and 450 required lane runs.
    34	- The current public state is legitimately `case-study`, not `public-v1`: `benchmark/results/summary.json` says 3 captured/passed lane runs and 447 missing.
    35	- The generated workspace layout is consistent: `package.json`, `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, lane-shaped `data/`, `src/workflow.mjs`, `src/portal-view.mjs`, `src/render-proof.mjs`, `tests/acceptance.test.mjs`, and `tests-before.log`.
    36	- AST-Bench target workspaces are symmetric in structure but intentionally asymmetric in data modeling: MongoDB uses document-shaped collections; Postgres uses normalized relational tables.
    37	- Metrics are approximate: transcript tokens are estimated as `ceil(bytes / 4)`, and retry signals are crude regex counts over transcript/test text.
    38	
    39	References:
    40	- [1] `package.json`: `proof:*`, `instrumented:*`, and `benchmark:*` script families; especially the fact that `benchmark:all` does not run `benchmark:run`.
    41	- [2] `scripts/benchmark-lib.mjs`: current AST-Bench prompt template (`You are running AST-Bench, the Agent Schema Tax Benchmark.`) and the prompt rules.
    42	- [3] `scripts/benchmark-run.mjs`: the actual agent launch shapes for Codex/Claude/Cursor and the artifact capture flow.
    43	- [4] `scripts/benchmark-prepare.mjs`: generated target workspace layout and the malformed acceptance-test pattern.
    44	- [5] `scripts/benchmark-score.mjs`, `scripts/benchmark-gates.mjs`, `scripts/benchmark-build-public-bundle.mjs`: current scoring/gating/public-bundle behavior and caveats.
    45	- [6] `scripts/run-instrumented-codex.mjs` and `scripts/prepare-instrumented-run.mjs`: the older seed replay flow using `codex exec`.
    46	- [7] `benchmark/specs/ast-bench-v1.json`: 25 tasks, 2 lanes, 3 agents, 3 repeats, 450 lane runs, and the task catalog.
    47	- [8] `benchmark/results/summary.json`: `case-study`, 3 captured/passed lane runs, 447 missing.
    48	- [9] `instrumented-agent-runs/order-exception-codex-v1/mongo/prompt.md` and `.../postgres/prompt.md`: seed prompt text for the verified replay.
    49	- [10] `benchmark/runs/ast-bench-v1/renewal-risk-workspace/codex/repeat-1/mongo/workspace/tests/acceptance.test.mjs`: the broken AST-Bench generated test that references `db` at module scope.
    50	
    51	## Task 2: Compare system-prompt language and identify conflicts/drift
    52	
    53	Outcome: success
    54	
    55	Preference signals:
    56	- The user asked whether the agents “speak the same language” and have “the same idea” -> future similar analysis should explicitly compare wording across prompt layers, not just summarize each layer independently.
    57	- The user asked for the “full picture” -> future similar work should include stale naming, framing drift, and contradictory assumptions across seed vs benchmark layers.
    58	
    59	Key steps:
    60	- Compared root product language (`README.md`, `CONTEXT.md`, `PRODUCT.md`, `docs/PRD.md`) with generated prompt text.
    61	- Compared current AST-Bench prompts to older seed replay prompts.
    62	- Checked whether the benchmark actually has a repo-owned prompt or just CLI prompt text.
    63	- Used read-only evidence from the generated public bundle and verified replay artifacts to understand what the public-facing system says vs what the runtime actually uses.
    64	
    65	Failures and how to do differently:
    66	- The seed replay prompt is stale relative to the later AST-Bench framing. It still says “MongoDB’s Interactive Proof Lab,” while the current repo language puts AST-Bench first and uses Interactive Proof Lab as the rendering/theater layer.
    67	- The fairness contract in docs is stronger than the prompt text itself: docs talk about same prompt, agent, model/version, time budget, fixture version, failed attempts, and raw evidence links, but the prompt string only covers task/acceptance/native-model/no-test-edit/no-simulation rules.
    68	- Captured Codex runs are not hermetic because the agent read `~/.codex/memories/MEMORY.md`; future fair comparisons should treat global agent memory/config as part of the environment contract.
    69	- Root demo metrics are inconsistent across docs (`README.md` vs `docs/seller-demo-script.md`), so public-facing numbers need careful source selection.
    70	
    71	Reusable knowledge:
    72	- Current AST-Bench prompt template: “You are running AST-Bench, the Agent Schema Tax Benchmark,” followed by task title, business request, acceptance criteria, native model rule, `npm test`, and “Do not hand-wave or simulate evidence.”
    73	- Older seed prompt template: “You are running an Instrumented Agent Run for MongoDB’s Interactive Proof Lab,” followed by the Customer 360 Escalation task for `HX-20491` / `acct-nova`, exact portal outcomes, and the “do not change tests/data” rule.
    74	- Generated `AGENTS.md` files are terse and task-specific; they are not the authoritative system prompt, but they do constrain implementation in each workspace.
    75	- The public bundle and no-mock gate encode stronger public-proof language than the seed prompts do.
    76	
    77	References:
    78	- [1] `scripts/benchmark-lib.mjs:172-192` current AST-Bench prompt template.
    79	- [2] `instrumented-agent-runs/order-exception-codex-v1/mongo/prompt.md:1-25` older seed prompt template.
    80	- [3] `benchmark/targets/strategic-account-rescue/mongo/workspace/AGENTS.md:1-8` and `.../postgres/...`: current generated target rules.

200	
   201	- There is no repo-owned root `AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, or hidden system prompt here; the current benchmark prompt is a CLI prompt string passed to `codex exec`, plus generated workspace files such as `AGENTS.md` and `README.md` [Task 1][Task 2]
   202	- `package.json` splits the repo into `proof:*`, `instrumented:*`, and `benchmark:*` families; `benchmark:all` is scoring/gating/presentation, not agent execution [Task 1]
   203	- AST-Bench V1 currently defines 25 tasks, 2 lanes, 3 agents, 3 repeats, and 450 required lane runs, while `benchmark/results/summary.json` still says `case-study` with 3 captured/passed lane runs and 447 missing, so this repo is not yet at `public-v1` proof breadth [Task 1]
   204	- Generated AST-Bench workspaces are symmetric in shape but intentionally asymmetric in data modeling: MongoDB uses document-shaped collections while Postgres uses normalized relational tables [Task 1]
   205	- The current AST-Bench prompt says "You are running AST-Bench, the Agent Schema Tax Benchmark" with task title, business request, acceptance criteria, native-model rule, `npm test`, and "Do not hand-wave or simulate evidence", while the older seed prompt still says "MongoDB's Interactive Proof Lab" and bakes in the Customer 360 Escalation task [Task 2]
   206	- Generated `AGENTS.md` files are terse task-local constraints rather than the authoritative prompt, and public fairness language in docs is stricter than the prompt string alone [Task 2]
   207	- `check-proof-gates` verifies evidence-manifest SHA-256 integrity, `promote-verified-replay` enforces the verified replay contract, and `build-public-evidence-bundle` packages those artifacts; none of those scripts independently prove that current autonomous execution is healthy [Task 3]
   208	
   209	## Failures and how to do differently
   210	
   211	- Symptom: the repo gets treated like a DeepAgents/LangGraph runtime; cause: benchmark scripts and generated workspaces are mistaken for an in-repo agent runtime; fix: start from `package.json`, `scripts/benchmark-run.mjs`, `scripts/benchmark-prepare.mjs`, and the generated targets before reasoning about autonomy [Task 1]
   212	- Symptom: AST-Bench results look like agent failures before workflow logic is even exercised; cause: generated acceptance tests leak `db` / `tables` globals that do not exist in test scope; fix: repair the harness/test generator before drawing autonomy conclusions, and treat `globalThis.db = db` workarounds as benchmark leakage rather than clean feature wins [Task 1]
   213	- Symptom: prompt-comparison work overstates sameness because the prompt string matches superficially; cause: generated `AGENTS.md`, docs, and global Codex memory/config are part of the real run environment; fix: compare the whole environment contract, not only the CLI prompt string [Task 2]
   214	- Symptom: a passed proof gate gets mistaken for healthy autonomous execution; cause: `benchmark-gates` and related scripts validate artifacts, manifests, and no-test-edit rules rather than exit codes, command counts, or transcript quality; fix: evaluate proof correctness and autonomy correctness as separate questions [Task 3]
   215

item.text:
Implemented the workflow so the Postgres target now persists the request-scoped state, routed owner tasks, customer-safe message, and customer-visible audit event from the normalized tables.

- [src/workflow.mjs](/Users/rom.iluz/Dev/sql-hidden-cost/benchmark/runs-v2/ast-bench-v2/deep/codex/repeat-5/postgres/workspace/src/workflow.mjs#L6) now joins `accounts`, `account_contracts`, `support_plans`, `invoice_risk`, `account_addresses`, `workflow_request_owner_groups`, `workflow_request_risk_signals`, `activities`, `contact_x_owner_group`, and `contacts`, then writes the workflow rows back into `workflow_state`, `owner_tasks`, `customer_messages`, and `audit_events`.
- [src/portal-view.mjs](/Users/rom.iluz/Dev/sql-hidden-cost/benchmark/runs-v2/ast-bench-v2/deep/codex/repeat-5/postgres/workspace/src/portal-view.mjs#L1) now reads the latest persisted state/message and keeps owner routing order stable.
- Verification passed with `npm test`, `npm run render`, and `git diff --check`.
